title: Urine on Wool — Time-Sensitive, pH-Sensitive, Fiber-Destroying If Mishandled
stainChemistry: Urine is a complex cocktail: urea, uric acid, ammonia, chloride salts, organic compounds, and the pigment urovilin (which causes the yellow color). Dan Eisen notes the color 'ranges from yellow to brown' and 'intensifies from age.' On wool, urine is particularly damaging because (1) the ammonia in urine is alkaline, which opens wool's keratin scales and promotes felting; (2) chloride salts can cause permanent dye damage on wool; (3) uric acid crystals embed in the fiber and resist standard cleaning — they reactivate with moisture, which is why old urine stains emit odor when re-wetted; (4) prolonged exposure causes actual fiber deterioration, not just staining.
whyThisWorks: The protocol addresses urine's components in the correct order: (1) cold water flush to dilute and remove water-soluble components before they set; (2) mild acid (vinegar) to neutralize the alkaline ammonia component and prevent wool scale damage; (3) NSD surfactant to emulsify the organic compounds; (4) careful ammonia application to saponify any fatty components (per Dan Eisen's procedure); (5) peroxide only if yellow chromophore persists. On wool, every step must stay below 30°C and minimize agitation. No protease enzymes — wool is keratin protein.

spottingProtocol.technique: Flush from back with water gun at safe distance
spottingProtocol.temperature: Cold — below 80°F / 27°C. Wool felts above 30°C when wet.
spottingProtocol.instruction: Place wool face-down on clean white towel over vacuum. Flush with cold water from back. Use gentle pressure — wet wool is fragile. You'll smell the urine reactivate as water hits it (Dan Eisen warns about this). Keep flushing until the towel shows minimal yellow transfer. The goal is removing the bulk of water-soluble urea and salts before they set.

spottingProtocol.agent: White vinegar (5% acetic acid), diluted 1:1 with cold water
spottingProtocol.technique: Apply with sponge, gentle press — no tamping
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply diluted vinegar to the stain area. The acetic acid serves two purposes: (1) neutralizes the alkaline ammonia in urine that would otherwise open wool scales and promote felting; (2) helps dissolve uric acid crystals. On wool, use 1:1 dilution — undiluted vinegar (pH ~2.4) is too acidic for prolonged wool contact. Gently press with sponge. Do not tamp or brush wet wool.
Flush the vinegar and dissolved urine components through. Inspect the towel — yellow transfer should be diminishing.

spottingProtocol.agent: NSD (neutral synthetic detergent) + 1–2 drops household ammonia
spottingProtocol.technique: Apply with soft pad, very gentle press
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply NSD solution with a small amount of household ammonia. Dan Eisen's urine procedure includes ammonia to address the organic compounds and enhance cleaning. On wool, use only 1–2 drops of household ammonia per tablespoon of NSD — keep the pH well below 8. Test dye safety in hidden area first. Ammonia can shift colors on wool, especially blues and greens. Gently press with pad.
Flush thoroughly. Dan Eisen alternates acid and alkali in his urine procedure — the alternating pH helps dislodge different stain components. At this point, most of the urine should be removed.

spottingProtocol.agent: Hydrogen peroxide 3% + 1 drop ammonia (ONLY if yellow remains)
spottingProtocol.instruction: Only if yellow urovilin pigment persists. Apply 3% peroxide (NOT 6% on wool) with minimal ammonia catalyst. Dan Eisen includes this peroxide+ammonia step in his procedure: 'Apply hydrogen peroxide and then follow with ammonia. Wait 5 minutes.' On wool, reduce dwell to 3 minutes and monitor for color shift. Flush with cold water.

spottingProtocol.agent: Vinegar rinse + reshape and flat dry
spottingProtocol.technique: Apply dilute vinegar, flush, reshape, dry flat
spottingProtocol.instruction: Final vinegar rinse to neutralize any alkaline residue and close wool scales. Dan Eisen's final step: 'Apply household vinegar, rinse with water.' Gently press excess water out with towel — never wring. Reshape wool to original dimensions. Lay flat on dry towel. Air dry at room temperature. Never hang wet wool.

homeSolutions: FRESH: Blot as much urine as possible immediately with clean white towels — press firmly, don't rub. Then sponge the area with cold water + white vinegar (equal parts). The vinegar neutralizes the ammonia and prevents wool damage. Blot again. Repeat. Air dry flat.

SET/DRIED: Sponge with cold water + vinegar solution to dissolve uric acid crystals. Then apply a small amount of enzyme-FREE wool wash (Eucalan, Soak, or Woolite) in cold water. Gently work with fingers — no rubbing, no wringing. Rinse cold. Vinegar final rinse. Lay flat to dry. Do NOT use enzyme detergents like Tide on wool — protease destroys wool fiber.

FOR ODOR: Dan Eisen recommends unscented Lysol spray for perspiration odor, and the same applies to urine: a light spray of unscented Lysol around (not saturating) the affected area after treatment. On wool, use sparingly — do not saturate.

WHEN TO STOP: If yellow staining or odor persists after two vinegar + NSD treatments, take to a professional. Old urine that has caused dye damage or fiber deterioration may not be fully recoverable — but a professional can minimize it.
materialWarnings: NEVER hot water on urine + wool — simultaneously sets protein components AND felts the wool. Double damage.
NEVER protease enzymes on wool — wool IS a protein fiber. Enzyme will digest the wool itself.
NEVER chlorine bleach on wool — destroys keratin structure, causes irreversible yellowing.
NEVER sodium percarbonate (OxiClean) on wool — alkaline pH + oxygen release damages wool protein structure.
NEVER agitate wet wool — felting is irreversible. No rubbing, no wringing, no machine washing.

NEVER leave urine on wool for extended periods — Dan Eisen warns that urine 'could cause color change in the fabric and even cause the fabric to deteriorate.' Urine's chloride salts and ammonia actively damage wool fiber over time.

professional.name: Street's Protein Formula
professional.use: Protein component of urine — dilute 1:1 for wool
professional.note: Mild alkaline protein spotter. On wool, keep dwell under 5 minutes and pH below 8. Test dye safety first. Flush and neutralize with acetic acid immediately after.

professional.name: Hydrogen Peroxide 3%
professional.use: Yellow urovilin pigment removal — if color persists after protein treatment
professional.note: Use only 3% on wool — never 6%. Cool temperature only. Brief contact (2–3 minutes). Ammonia catalyst: 1 drop per tablespoon. Monitor for color shift.

professional.name: Prochem CLENSAN or MICROSAN
professional.note: Apply first if urine contamination is extensive. Dwell 15 minutes for antimicrobial action per Prochem protocol. Then proceed with stain removal.

consumer.name: White Distilled Vinegar
consumer.use: CRITICAL first step — neutralizes ammonia, dissolves uric acid, safe for wool
consumer.note: Dilute 1:1 with cold water for wool. Apply as first treatment. This is the single most important consumer product for urine on wool.

consumer.name: Eucalan or Soak Wool Wash
consumer.use: Enzyme-free, pH-neutral wool detergent for gentle cleaning
consumer.note: No-rinse formula designed for wool and delicates. No protease enzymes. Cold water only. Soak 15 minutes, squeeze gently, dry flat.

consumer.name: Unscented Lysol Spray
consumer.note: Dan Eisen's recommendation for perspiration/urine odor. Light spray only — do NOT saturate wool. Apply after the fabric is dry.

escalation.when: Yellow stain persists after two full treatment cycles, urine has been on wool for more than 48 hours, you notice dye damage (color change around the stain area), the wool shows signs of fiber deterioration (thinning, rough texture), or the odor persists after treatment.
escalation.whatToTell: Tell the cleaner: 'This is urine on wool. I treated with cold water and vinegar to neutralize the ammonia. I did not use enzymes, hot water, or bleach. The stain is approximately [X] old. There may be dye damage from the chloride salts in the urine — please assess before applying any alkaline chemistry.'
escalation.specialistType: Dry cleaner with wet cleaning capability — urine is a water-soluble stain that needs wet-side treatment. The cleaner should have experience with protein fibers and understand wool's pH and temperature limits. For rugs, refer to a rug cleaning specialist — wall-to-wall methods don't apply to wool area rugs.

solventNote: For dry cleaners: Urine on wool requires wet-side pre-spotting BEFORE machine cleaning. Drycleaning solvent removes only the fatty/organic portion — the water-soluble salts, urea, and uric acid remain and will cause continued damage. Pre-spot with diluted protein formula (keep pH below 8), flush cold, neutralize with acetic acid. Hydrocarbon solvent is preferred over PERC for wool. For persistent odor after cleaning, add a small amount of white vinegar to the final extraction rinse. GreenEarth or Solvon K4 are gentlest on wool and won't risk felting.
